skill.name: wordpress-router
skill.description: Classifies WordPress repositories and routes tasks to the right workflow for plugins, themes, Gutenberg blocks, WP-CLI, testing, security, and release work.
skill.summary: Route WordPress codebase tasks to the correct workflow and guardrails.
skill.author: Automattic

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command patterns and two weak cryptography patterns. Review found the weak cryptography alerts are false positives, and most command alerts are markdown identifiers or routing labels. One legitimate workflow risk remains because the skill instructs the agent to run a fixed local Node triage command.

medium_findings.title: Local Triage Command Execution Recommended
medium_findings.description: The skill directs the agent to run a fixed Node triage command. This appears related to the stated WordPress routing purpose, but it still asks the agent to execute a local command in the user workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is direct evidence of a command the agent is told to run. The command is fixed and purpose-aligned, so the risk is bounded rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command and Identifier False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are markdown code spans naming project kinds, files, route targets, or WordPress concepts. These references do not execute by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are markdown routing instructions and identifiers, not executable code. No dynamic shell construction or user-controlled command invocation is present there.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography alerts point to descriptive text and a section heading. No hash function, cipher, password storage logic, or cryptographic operation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain the WordPress scope description and a failure modes heading. They do not contain cryptographic APIs or algorithm names.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent-Instructed External Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes an instruction to run a Node-based project triage command. Users should review command execution in untrusted repositories before allowing it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and fixed. It is not evidence of malware, but external command execution is a real operational risk category.

content.user_title: Route WordPress Tasks to the Right Workflow
content.value_statement: WordPress codebases can be plugins, themes, block themes, full sites, or core checkouts. This skill classifies the repository and routes the task to focused workflows with practical guardrails.
actual_capabilities: Guides initial triage for WordPress repositories using a project detection script.
Classifies projects as plugins, themes, block themes, full sites, core checkouts, or Gutenberg workspaces.
Routes user intent to matching WordPress domain workflows and skills.
Lists precedence rules when a repository matches multiple WordPress project types.
Applies guardrails for version constraints, existing tooling, and test selection.
limitations: It does not include the referenced triage script inside this skill package.
It depends on related WordPress workflow skills being available for full routing.
It cannot inspect a repository without filesystem access to the project root.
Some referenced routes are marked as planned and may not be implemented yet.

use_cases.title: Classify an unfamiliar WordPress repository
use_cases.description: Identify whether a repository is a plugin, theme, block theme, full site, core checkout, or Gutenberg workspace before editing.

use_cases.title: Route a feature request to the right workflow
use_cases.description: Map user intent such as blocks, theme.json, REST, WP-CLI, testing, performance, or security to the best next workflow.

use_cases.title: Choose verification steps for WordPress changes
use_cases.description: Use detected tooling and project type to select lint, build, test, or WP-CLI checks that match the repository.

prompt_templates.title: Classify this WordPress project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the WordPress router to classify this repository. Tell me the project type, strongest signals, and the workflow I should use next.

prompt_templates.title: Route my WordPress task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the WordPress router for this request: [describe task]. Identify the likely WordPress area and route it to the best workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Plan verification for a WordPress change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the WordPress router to inspect the project type and available tooling. Recommend the safest lint, build, and test checks for my change.

prompt_templates.title: Resolve ambiguous WordPress routing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the WordPress router on this mixed repository. Explain every matching project kind, apply the precedence rules, and ask one blocking question if needed.

output_examples.input: I opened a repository with wp-content, a custom theme, and several plugins. Where should I start?
output_examples.output: The router would classify it as a full WordPress site first, then route the exact task to theme or plugin workflows.

output_examples.input: I need to change block.json attributes and save markup.
output_examples.output: The router would send the task to block development, then recommend checking build tooling and serialization-sensitive tests.

output_examples.input: The repository has theme.json and templates stored as HTML files.
output_examples.output: The router would treat it as a block theme task and prioritize global styles, templates, patterns, and version constraints.

best_practices: Run routing before editing when the WordPress project type is unclear.
Use the repository existing Composer, npm, yarn, pnpm, PHPUnit, Playwright, or wp-env setup when verifying changes.
Confirm target WordPress and PHP versions when repository metadata does not make them clear.
anti_patterns: Do not assume every WordPress repository is only a plugin or only a theme.
Do not run broad build or test commands before checking the project tooling.
Do not route security, REST, performance, or block work to generic WordPress advice when focused workflows exist.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It helps an AI assistant classify a WordPress repository and choose the correct workflow for the user request.

faq.question: Does it modify WordPress code directly?
faq.answer: No. It routes the task and defines guardrails before another workflow or skill performs implementation work.

faq.question: Does it require WP-CLI?
faq.answer: Some downstream workflows may require WP-CLI, but this router first checks what tooling appears available.

faq.question: Can it handle Gutenberg repositories?
faq.answer: Yes. The decision tree includes a Gutenberg route and gives it the highest precedence when multiple types match.

faq.question: Why is the security risk marked medium?
faq.answer: The skill recommends running a fixed local Node command. That is useful for triage but still requires command execution review.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
